macro_segments.title: Financial penalties for spousal non-compliance: The framework of reduction and increase

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the sugya: Marital disputes, compliance mechanisms, and the question of compulsion to divorce
The mishnah's penalty structure: Seven dinarin/tarpikin reduction for the wife, three for the husband
Conversion of currencies: Dinar, zuz, sela, shekel, and the two-hundred zuz kesuvah baseline
Calculation of penalty duration: Weekly deductions reducing the kesuvah over weeks and months
Definition of tarpikin as asteira (half-zuz) and the meaning of ma'in (daily measure)

macro_segments.title: Asymmetry in penalties: Why she is fined double and on Shabbat, while he is fined half and not on Shabbat

micro_segments.title: The gemara's logical challenge: Why different reduction amounts for mored versus moredet?
Why she is fined on Shabbat (debt reduction, not wages) and why he is not (would appear as Shabbat earnings)
The marketplace of prostitutes: Evidence for differential sexual injury and asymmetrical fining
Rashi's two interpretations: Greater male sexual desire and the visibility of male physiology
Summary of asymmetries: Double penalty for the wife, exclusion of Shabbat for the husband, and broader imbalances

macro_segments.title: Minimum provisions for a wife maintained separately: Food, household goods, and pin money

micro_segments.title: The mishnah's opening: Providing for the wife through an intermediary when not sharing domicile
Weekly grain allowance: Two kav of wheat or four kav of barley; Rabbi Yosi on Rabbi Ishmael's view
Additional consumables: Legumes, oil, dried figs or pressed dates—the barebones diet
Durable goods: Bed, mat, cushion, headcovering, belt, and shoes seasonally
Seasonal clothing: New clothes in winter, worn clothes reused in summer to avoid excess warmth
Pin money (ma'ah kesef) for miscellaneous needs, and the Friday night meal requirement
If he fails to provide pin money, she retains the income from her work (ma'aseh yadayim)

macro_segments.title: The woman's work obligation: Measuring the ma'aseh yadayim (weaving or earning work)

micro_segments.title: Definition of ma'aseh yadayim: Domestic duties versus earning work—ambiguity in the mishnah
Weaving quotas: Five sela'im of warp or ten sela'im of woof per week (Judah vs. Galilee measures)

macro_segments.title: Minimum provisions for a wife maintained separately: Food, household goods, and pin money (continued)

micro_segments.title: Nursing mother: Reduced work obligations and increased food allowance
Limitation to the poor (ani she'b'Yisrael): All according to his status for the wealthy

macro_segments.title: The two-meals standard and its calculation: Bread measures from Rabbi Yochanan ben Broka and Rabbi Shimon

micro_segments.title: The eruv techumin law: Two meals per person as the standard for shared food provisions
Dispute on measurement: Weekday bread (Rabbi Meir) versus Shabbat bread (Rabbi Yehuda)
Rabbi Yochanan ben Broka: Half a kav of wheat makes one loaf equaling two meals
Rabbi Shimon: Two-thirds loaf from three loaves per kav, equaling two-ninths kav for two meals
The problem: Two kav yields only eight meals (RYbB) or eighteen meals (R. Shimon), but fourteen are needed weekly
Resolution via baker's profit: Factoring in markup (one-third or one-half) to reconcile the wheat amount
Adjustments for Shabbat meals and guests: Reaching twelve, thirteen, sixteen, or eighteen total meals

macro_segments.title: Wine allocation for the separated wife: Restrictions, exceptions, and concerns about impropriety

micro_segments.title: No wine in the standard provisions: Rabbi Elazar's rule that beis din does not allocate wine
Proof from Chana: Textual drasha suggesting she did not drink wine when separated from Elkanah
Baraita exception: If the wife is accustomed to wine, one cup is allocated (or two if not accustomed)
Abaye's refined reading: One cup if she normally drinks two with her husband; none if she normally drinks one
Wine for cooking (l'tziki kdera) is permitted even without the husband present
Tannaitic warning: One cup is good; two is unseemly; three leads to demanding sex; four to bestiality
Rava's limitation: All restrictions apply only when the husband is absent; no restrictions when together

macro_segments.title: Narrative examples: The cases of Choma (Abaye's wife), Rava's wife, and geographical context

micro_segments.title: Choma's petition: A beautiful woman comes to Rava for mezonot allocation and requests wine
Rava's response: Wine is allocated based on Nachmani's abstinence, contrasted with her account of Nachmani's actual drinking
Rava's distraction: As Choma demonstrates drinking from a large cup, her arm becomes exposed and glows
Rava's immediate reaction: Disturbed and sexually aroused, he goes home and initiates relations with his wife
His wife's response: She chases Choma from Mechuza with a strap, accusing her of causing three husbands' deaths
A second example: Rav Yosef's wife receives wine allocation from Rav Nechemya based on local custom in Mechuza

topical_tags.term: Shabbat wage concerns and debt reduction
Kedei achilat pras (time to eat half a loaf)
Rabbi Elazar (no wine for separated wife)
Tannaitic teaching on wine and female behavior
